Fig. 6 (A): Representation of Constructed Graph in Delta Band, (B): Representation of Constructed Graph in Theta Band Though these graphs might seem different at first glance, the nodes and connections are found to be consistent throughout. This graph representation allows the GCN to perform convolutions not just on the signal features themselves, but also across the network's topology, thereby learning from both local node information and the broader connectivity of the brain.

A typical layout of a Graph Convolutional Network 𝐻(0) = 𝑘 (21) Except for the neighborhood averaging step applied at the beginning of each layer (Figure 7), a Klayer GCN can be viewed as a K-layer MLP operating on each node feature vector xix_ixi within the graph. In each graph convolution layer, node embeddings are updated through three main operations: feature propagation, linear transformation, and element-wise nonlinear activation. The details of these operations are explained below for clarity.
